1:
"We then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak, and not to please ourselves." The witnesses note the duty of the strong to bear with the weak. On "we that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak," the grammatical voices note the obligation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, identifying himself with the strong (those of robust faith and full liberty), lays down their duty: to bear (carry, support, endure patiently) the infirmities (the scruples and weaknesses) of the weak — not to crush but to uphold them.

On "not to please ourselves," the witnesses note the principle of self-denial. Barnes and Calvin note the strong are not to consult their own gratification or insist on their own liberty, but to deny themselves for the good of the weaker brother.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the law of love: strength is given not for self-pleasing but for service; the strong owe it to the weak to support them, accommodating themselves to their infirmity. The verse displays the duty of the strong to bear with the weak.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, identifying himself with the strong (those of robust faith and full liberty), lays down their duty — to bear (support, endure patiently) the infirmities of the weak, not to please themselves (Barnes, Robertson); the strong are not to consult their own gratification but to deny themselves for the weaker brother's good (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Calvin note strength is given for service, not self-pleasing. The verse displays the duty of the strong. The verse marks the obligation: bear the infirmities. The verse displays the self-denial: not please ourselves. The verse displays the law: love. The verse displays the use of strength: service. The duty displays the self-denial owed by the strong to the weak. The verse displays the bearing: of infirmities. The verse displays the denial: of self. The verse displays the service: to the weak. The duty of the strong to bear with the weak — to bear their infirmities and not please ourselves — displays the self-denial that love requires of those endowed with strength: their robust faith is given not for self-gratification but to uphold and accommodate the weaker brother. The verse opens the chapter's exhortation to mutual forbearance, continuing the theme of ch. 14, and directs the strong to seek the neighbour's good (v.2).
2:
"Let every one of us please his neighbour for his good to edification." The brief verse notes pleasing the neighbour for his edification. The grammatical voices note the rule. Barnes and Robertson note Paul directs each to please (seek the satisfaction and good of) his neighbour — but not in flattery or sin; rather "for his good," with a view to his edification (building up).

On "for his good to edification," the witnesses note the limit and aim. The pleasing is qualified: not to gratify the neighbour in evil, but to seek his true good and spiritual upbuilding — the standard of self-accommodation is the brother's edification.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the balance: we are to accommodate ourselves to others, yet always toward their edification, not mere man-pleasing; love seeks the neighbour's profit. The verse displays pleasing the neighbour for his good and edification.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul directs each to please (seek the good of) his neighbour — not in flattery or sin, but "for his good," with a view to his edification (Barnes, Robertson); the pleasing qualified by the brother's true good and spiritual upbuilding, the standard being edification. Calvin and Theodoret note we are to accommodate others, yet always toward their edification, not mere man-pleasing. The verse displays pleasing the neighbour for his good. The verse marks the rule: please his neighbour. The verse displays the limit: for his good. The verse displays the aim: edification. The verse displays the balance: not man-pleasing. The rule displays self-accommodation aimed at the neighbour's upbuilding. The verse displays the pleasing: of the neighbour. The verse displays the good: his true profit. The verse displays the building: edification. Pleasing the neighbour for his good and edification displays the principle of loving self-accommodation: each is to seek the satisfaction of his neighbour, not to gratify him in evil but for his true good and spiritual upbuilding. The verse displays the constructive aim that governs the strong's self-denial, and grounds it in the supreme example of Christ (v.3). The standard is thus never the mere gratification of the neighbour but his solid and lasting upbuilding, so that even our yielding becomes a means of his growth in faith rather than a flattery of his weakness.
3:
"For even Christ pleased not himself; but, as it is written, The reproaches of them that reproached thee fell on me." The witnesses note Christ the supreme example of self-denial. On "even Christ pleased not himself," the grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds the duty of self-denial in Christ's own example: Christ, the highest, did not please himself — he sought not his own ease or honour but the Father's will and our salvation.

On the citation "the reproaches... fell on me," the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Calvin note Paul quotes Psalm 69:9 (a messianic psalm): the reproaches against God fell upon Christ — he bore, in his suffering and humiliation, the insults aimed at God, taking them on himself for our sake.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the force: if Christ, the Lord of all, denied himself so far as to bear reproach for others, how much more should we, his servants, forgo our own pleasure for our brethren. The verse displays Christ the supreme example of self-denial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grounds the duty of self-denial in Christ's own example — Christ, the highest, did not please himself but sought the Father's will and our salvation (Barnes, Robertson) — proved by Psalm 69:9 (a messianic psalm): the reproaches against God fell upon Christ, who bore the insults aimed at God for our sake (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Theodoret note the force: if Christ denied himself to bear reproach for others, how much more should we forgo our pleasure for our brethren. The verse displays Christ the supreme example. The verse marks the example: Christ pleased not himself. The verse displays the proof: the reproaches fell on him. The verse displays the citation: Psalm 69. The verse displays the force: how much more we. Christ's example displays the pattern of self-denial for others. The verse displays the self-denial: of Christ. The verse displays the reproach: borne for us. The verse displays the lesson: imitate him. Christ the supreme example of self-denial — he pleased not himself but bore the reproaches aimed at God — displays the pattern that binds his servants: if the Lord of all denied himself to suffer for others, his people must forgo their own pleasure for their brethren. The verse displays the example of Christ as the ground of all Christian self-denial, and turns aside to commend the abiding profit of the Scriptures (v.4).
4:
"For whatsoever things were written aforetime were written for our learning, that we through patience and comfort of the scriptures might have hope." The witnesses note the abiding profit of the Old Testament Scriptures. On "whatsoever things were written aforetime, for our learning," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke note Paul, having quoted the Psalm, turns aside to a great truth: all the Old Testament Scriptures were written for our instruction — designed by God for the teaching of all later generations, not only the age that first received them.

On "through patience and comfort of the scriptures," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note "patience and comfort (consolation) of the Scriptures" means the patience and consolation which the Scriptures supply — by their promises, examples, and consolations sustaining the believer under trial.

On "might have hope," the Reformed Calvin notes the end: the special use of Scripture is to nourish hope — raising up, through patience and consolation, the hope of eternal life. Calvin adds a sharp polemic: this condemns the fanatics who say the Old Testament is abolished and belongs not to Christians, for Paul testifies it was appointed by God for our salvation. The verse displays the abiding profit of the Scriptures.

The witnesses agree: all the Old Testament Scriptures were written for our instruction — designed by God for all later generations (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke) — that "through patience and comfort of the Scriptures" (the patience and consolation the Scriptures supply, by their promises and examples sustaining the believer, Barnes, Calvin) we might have hope, the nourishing of the hope of eternal life being their special use. Calvin sharply condemns the fanatics who say the Old Testament is abolished, since Paul testifies it was appointed for our salvation. The verse displays the abiding profit of Scripture. The verse marks the principle: written for our learning. The verse displays the means: patience and comfort of the Scriptures. The verse displays the end: hope. The verse displays the polemic: against discarding the OT. The abiding profit displays the divine design of Scripture for our instruction and hope. The verse displays the writing: for our learning. The verse displays the consolation: from the Scriptures. The verse displays the hope: nourished. The abiding profit of the Old Testament Scriptures — written aforetime for our learning, that through their patience and comfort we might have hope — displays the divine design of all Scripture for the instruction and hope of every generation: God appointed the ancient writings to sustain the believer under trial and to nourish the hope of eternal life. The verse displays the perpetual usefulness of the whole of Scripture, against any who would discard the Old Testament, and prays for the likemindedness it teaches (v.5).
5:
"Now the God of patience and consolation grant you to be likeminded one toward another according to Christ Jesus:" The witnesses note the prayer for likemindedness from the God of patience. On "the God of patience and consolation," the grammatical voices note the title. Barnes and Robertson note Paul names God as the author and giver of the patience and consolation just mentioned (v.4) — the source of the graces the Scriptures convey.

On "grant you to be likeminded one toward another," the witnesses note the petition. Barnes and Calvin note Paul prays for unity of mind among them — that the strong and weak be of one accord, harmonious in spirit, despite their differences over meats and days.

On "according to Christ Jesus," the Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the standard: the likemindedness is to be according to Christ — patterned on his example and will, in conformity to him, not a mere worldly agreement. The verse displays the prayer for likemindedness from the God of patience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul names God as the author and giver of the patience and consolation just mentioned (Barnes, Robertson) and prays for unity of mind — that the strong and weak be of one accord despite their differences (Barnes, Calvin) — "according to Christ Jesus," patterned on his example and will, in conformity to him (Calvin, Henry). The verse displays the prayer for likemindedness. The verse marks the title: the God of patience. The verse displays the petition: to be likeminded. The verse displays the standard: according to Christ. The verse displays the source: God the giver. The prayer displays the divine source of Christian unity. The verse displays the God: of patience and consolation. The verse displays the unity: likemindedness. The verse displays the pattern: Christ. The prayer for likemindedness from the God of patience displays the divine source of Christian unity: Paul asks God, the author of the patience and consolation the Scriptures convey, to grant the strong and weak to be of one accord, patterned according to Christ Jesus. The verse displays that harmony among brethren is God's gift, sought in prayer, and aims at the united glorifying of God (v.6).
6:
"That ye may with one mind and one mouth glorify God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the goal of united worship. On "with one mind and one mouth glorify God," the grammatical voices note the aim. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of the likemindedness (v.5): that with one accord (one mind) and one voice (one mouth) they may glorify God — unanimous, harmonious praise, the fruit of unity.

On "the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the object of worship. Barnes notes God is glorified as the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ — the distinctive Christian designation; worship offered to the Father through and in relation to the Son.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed note the bearing: the end of all forbearance and unity among brethren is the common, harmonious glorifying of God; divisions mar his praise, concord magnifies it. The verse displays the goal of united worship.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of the likemindedness is that with one accord (one mind) and one voice (one mouth) they may glorify God — unanimous, harmonious praise, the fruit of unity (Barnes, Robertson) — God glorified as "the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," the distinctive Christian designation, worship offered to the Father in relation to the Son. Cyril notes the end of all forbearance is the common glorifying of God, divisions marring his praise. The verse displays the goal of united worship. The verse marks the aim: glorify God. The verse displays the unity: one mind, one mouth. The verse displays the object: the Father of our Lord. The verse displays the end: concord magnifying praise. The goal displays united worship as the end of Christian unity. The verse displays the one mind: concord. The verse displays the one mouth: harmonious praise. The verse displays the God: the Father. The goal of united worship — that with one mind and one mouth they may glorify God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ — displays the end toward which all forbearance and unity tend: the harmonious, unanimous praise of God by a reconciled people. The verse displays that concord among brethren exists for the glory of God, and grounds the mutual reception in Christ's own (v.7).
7:
"Wherefore receive ye one another, as Christ also received us to the glory of God." The witnesses note the command to receive one another as Christ received us. On "receive ye one another," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul sums up the exhortation: the strong and weak are to receive (welcome, embrace in fellowship) one another — mutual acceptance across their differences.

On "as Christ also received us," the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note the measure of this reception: as Christ received us — graciously, freely, while we were unworthy, Jew and Gentile alike. Christ's reception of us, undeserving as we were, is the model and motive for our receiving one another.

On "to the glory of God," the puritan Henry and eastern Theodoret note the end: Christ received us (and we are to receive one another) for the glory of God — mutual acceptance, like Christ's, redounds to God's praise. The verse displays the command to receive one another as Christ received us.

The witnesses agree: Paul sums up the exhortation — the strong and weak are to receive (welcome in fellowship) one another (Barnes, Robertson) — by the measure of Christ's own reception of us: graciously, freely, while we were unworthy, Jew and Gentile alike (Barnes, Calvin); Christ's welcome of the undeserving the model and motive for ours. Henry and Theodoret note the end: Christ received us, and we receive one another, to the glory of God. The verse displays the command to receive one another. The verse marks the duty: receive one another. The verse displays the pattern: as Christ received us. The verse displays the grace: while unworthy. The verse displays the end: the glory of God. The command displays Christ's reception as the measure of ours. The verse displays the welcome: mutual. The verse displays the model: Christ. The verse displays the praise: God's glory. The command to receive one another as Christ received us displays the gracious pattern of Christian acceptance: as Christ welcomed us, freely and while we were unworthy, so the strong and weak are to embrace one another across their differences, to the glory of God. The verse displays the climax of the exhortation to forbearance, and unfolds how Christ received both Jew and Gentile (vv.8-12).
8:
"Now I say that Jesus Christ was a minister of the circumcision for the truth of God, to confirm the promises made unto the fathers:" The witnesses note Christ the minister of the circumcision confirming the promises. On "a minister of the circumcision," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul affirms that Christ (the Messiah) exercised his earthly ministry among the Jews ("the circumcision") — born a Jew, sent to them, ministering in their midst.

On "for the truth of God, to confirm the promises to the fathers," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Chrysostom note Christ came to the Jews to vindicate the truth (faithfulness) of God — to confirm and fulfil the promises made to the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob); his coming proved God true to his covenant word.

The Reformed Calvin notes the doctrine: since Christ fulfilled the promises made to the fathers, the salvation he brought belonged by covenant to the Jews, and the old covenant was therefore spiritual, not merely temporal — against those who reduce the OT promises to earthly things. The verse displays Christ the minister of the circumcision confirming the promises.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ exercised his earthly ministry among the Jews ("the circumcision"), born a Jew and sent to them (Barnes, Robertson), to vindicate the truth (faithfulness) of God by confirming and fulfilling the promises made to the patriarchs (Barnes, Chrysostom); his coming proved God true to his covenant. Calvin notes that since Christ fulfilled the promises to the fathers, salvation belonged by covenant to the Jews and the old covenant was spiritual, not merely temporal. The verse displays Christ the minister of the circumcision. The verse marks the office: minister of the circumcision. The verse displays the purpose: the truth of God. The verse displays the confirmation: the promises to the fathers. The verse displays the doctrine: the covenant spiritual. Christ the minister displays God's faithfulness to his covenant promises. The verse displays the ministry: to the Jews. The verse displays the truth: God faithful. The verse displays the promises: confirmed. Christ the minister of the circumcision, confirming the promises made to the fathers, displays God's faithfulness to his covenant: Christ came to the Jews to vindicate the truth of God by fulfilling the promises to the patriarchs, so that salvation belonged by covenant to them first. The verse displays the first design of Christ's coming (to the Jews, for God's truth), and adds the second (to the Gentiles, for God's mercy, vv.9-12).
9:
"And that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy; as it is written, For this cause I will confess to thee among the Gentiles, and sing unto thy name." The witnesses note the Gentiles glorifying God for his mercy. On "that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy," the grammatical voices note the second design. Barnes and Chrysostom note the contrast with v.8: while Christ came to the Jews for God's truth (the promises), he came to the Gentiles for God's mercy — the Gentiles, having no covenant promises, are saved by pure mercy, and so glorify God for it.

On the citation, the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul begins a chain of Old Testament quotations showing the Gentiles' calling foretold; this first (Psalm 18:49) speaks of confessing God and singing his name among the Gentiles — the praise of God spreading to the nations.

The eastern Theodoret notes the humbling point (with Chrysostom): the Gentiles owe their salvation to mercy alone, not promise — a ground for humility, and a reason to bear with the weak Jews who had the promises. The verse displays the Gentiles glorifying God for his mercy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: in contrast with v.8 (Christ to the Jews for God's truth), Christ came to the Gentiles for God's mercy — the Gentiles, having no covenant promises, saved by pure mercy and so glorifying God for it (Barnes, Chrysostom); and Paul begins a chain of OT quotations showing this foretold, the first (Psalm 18:49) speaking of confessing God and singing among the Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson). Theodoret notes the Gentiles owe their salvation to mercy alone, a ground for humility. The verse displays the Gentiles glorifying God for mercy. The verse marks the design: mercy to the Gentiles. The verse displays the contrast: truth to Jews, mercy to Gentiles. The verse displays the citation: Psalm 18. The verse displays the humility: mercy alone. The Gentiles glorifying God display the calling of the nations by mercy. The verse displays the mercy: to the Gentiles. The verse displays the praise: among the nations. The verse displays the foretelling: the Scriptures. The Gentiles glorifying God for his mercy — confessing him among the nations and singing his name — display the second design of Christ's coming: the Gentiles, lacking the covenant promises, are saved by pure mercy and called to praise God for it, as the Scriptures foretold. The verse displays the foretold calling of the Gentiles, and continues the prophetic chain (vv.10-12).
10:
"And again he saith, Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people." The brief verse continues the prophetic chain on the Gentiles' calling. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the second quotation (Deuteronomy 32:43): "Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people" — the Gentiles called to rejoice together with God's people (Israel), foretelling their joint participation in salvation.

On "with his people," the witnesses note the union. The Gentiles are to rejoice alongside Israel — not separately but together, the two joined in one people of God, sharing the same joy.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the prophecy foretells the union of Jew and Gentile in common rejoicing — a ground for their mutual reception and concord. The verse displays the Gentiles called to rejoice with God's people.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second quotation (Deuteronomy 32:43) — "Rejoice, ye Gentiles, with his people" — calls the Gentiles to rejoice together with God's people Israel, foretelling their joint participation in salvation (Barnes, Robertson); the Gentiles rejoicing alongside Israel, the two joined in one people sharing the same joy. Calvin and Wesley note the prophecy foretells the union of Jew and Gentile in common rejoicing, a ground for mutual reception. The verse displays the Gentiles rejoicing with God's people. The verse marks the citation: Deuteronomy. The verse displays the call: rejoice, ye Gentiles. The verse displays the union: with his people. The verse displays the point: Jew and Gentile joined. The citation displays the foretold union of Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the rejoicing: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the togetherness: with Israel. The verse displays the concord: one people. The Gentiles called to rejoice with God's people displays the foretold union of Jew and Gentile in the one salvation: the prophecy bids the nations rejoice together with Israel, joined in common joy as one people of God. The verse displays the scriptural ground for the concord of Jew and Gentile, and adds a third witness (v.11). That the nations should be summoned to share the very joy of Israel, and not merely to look on from afar, shows how thoroughly the ancient Scriptures had already foreseen the breaking down of the wall between the two peoples.
11:
"And again, Praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles; and laud him, all ye people." The brief verse adds a third prophetic witness to the Gentiles' praise. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the third quotation (Psalm 117:1): "Praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles; and laud (extol) him, all ye people" — a universal call to all nations to praise God.

On "all ye Gentiles... all ye people," the witnesses note the universality. The repetition ("all ye Gentiles," "all ye people") underscores the breadth — every nation summoned to the praise of the Lord, the gospel embracing all peoples.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the force: the prophets repeatedly foretold the Gentiles' calling to praise God; the accumulation of witnesses puts the matter beyond doubt. The verse displays the universal call of the Gentiles to praise.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third quotation (Psalm 117:1) — "Praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles; and laud him, all ye people" — is a universal call to all nations to praise God (Barnes, Robertson); the repetition underscoring the breadth, every nation summoned to praise, the gospel embracing all peoples. Theodoret and Calvin note the prophets repeatedly foretold the Gentiles' calling, the accumulation of witnesses putting it beyond doubt. The verse displays the universal call to praise. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 117. The verse displays the call: praise the Lord. The verse displays the breadth: all ye Gentiles. The verse displays the force: repeated witness. The citation displays the universal summons of the nations to praise. The verse displays the praise: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the universality: all peoples. The verse displays the accumulation: many witnesses. The universal call of the Gentiles to praise — praise the Lord, all ye Gentiles, and laud him, all ye people — displays the breadth of the gospel's embrace: every nation is summoned to the praise of God, the prophets repeatedly foretelling the calling of the Gentiles. The verse displays the abundant scriptural witness to the nations' worship, and crowns the chain with the root of Jesse (v.12).
12:
"And again, Esaias saith, There shall be a root of Jesse, and he that shall rise to reign over the Gentiles; in him shall the Gentiles trust." The witnesses note the root of Jesse reigning over the Gentiles. On "a root of Jesse," the grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the climactic quotation (Isaiah 11:10): the "root of Jesse" is the Messiah, the descendant of Jesse (David's father) — Christ, sprung from the royal line.

On "he that shall rise to reign over the Gentiles," the witnesses note the dominion. Barnes notes the Messiah would rise (in his resurrection and exaltation) to reign over the Gentiles — his kingdom extending to all nations, not Israel only.

On "in him shall the Gentiles trust," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the faith of the nations: the Gentiles would hope in (trust, believe on) Christ — the object of their faith and the ground of their salvation. The verse displays the root of Jesse reigning over the Gentiles, the object of their trust.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the climactic quotation (Isaiah 11:10) — the "root of Jesse" is the Messiah, Christ sprung from David's line (Barnes, Robertson) — who would rise (in resurrection and exaltation) to reign over the Gentiles, his kingdom extending to all nations (Barnes); and "in him shall the Gentiles trust," the nations hoping in and believing on Christ as the object of their faith (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the root of Jesse reigning over the Gentiles. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 11. The verse displays the Messiah: the root of Jesse. The verse displays the dominion: reigning over the Gentiles. The verse displays the faith: the Gentiles trust. The root of Jesse displays Christ's reign and the Gentiles' faith foretold. The verse displays the root: of Jesse. The verse displays the reign: over the Gentiles. The verse displays the trust: in him. The root of Jesse reigning over the Gentiles, in whom they trust, displays the climactic prophecy of Christ's universal kingdom: the Messiah, sprung from David's line, rises to reign over all nations, who hope in him for salvation. The verse displays the foretold reign of Christ over the believing Gentiles, crowning the chain of witnesses, and breaks into a prayer for their joy and hope (v.13).
13:
"Now the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the prayer to the God of hope for joy, peace, and abounding hope. On "the God of hope," the grammatical voices note the title. Barnes and Robertson note Paul calls God "the God of hope" — the author and giver of hope, fitly named after the prophecies of the Gentiles' hope in Christ (v.12).

On "fill you with all joy and peace in believing," the witnesses note the petition. Barnes and Henry note Paul prays that God would fill them with all joy and peace — and this "in believing," for faith is the means by which joy and peace are received; the blessings flow through believing.

On "that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the end and agent: the joy and peace overflow into abounding hope, all wrought by the power of the Holy Spirit — hope is no human achievement but the Spirit's work. The verse displays the prayer to the God of hope for joy, peace, and abounding hope.

The witnesses agree: Paul calls God "the God of hope" (the author and giver of hope, fitly named after the prophecies of the Gentiles' hope, Barnes, Robertson) and prays that God would fill them with all joy and peace "in believing" — faith the means by which these blessings are received (Barnes, Henry) — "that ye may abound in hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost," the joy and peace overflowing into abounding hope, all wrought by the Spirit (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays the prayer to the God of hope. The verse marks the title: the God of hope. The verse displays the petition: joy and peace in believing. The verse displays the end: abounding hope. The verse displays the agent: the Holy Ghost. The prayer displays joy, peace, and hope as the Spirit's gift through faith. The verse displays the joy: filling. The verse displays the peace: in believing. The verse displays the hope: abounding. The prayer to the God of hope for joy, peace, and abounding hope, through the power of the Holy Ghost, displays the Spirit-wrought blessings that flow through faith: Paul asks God to fill the believers with joy and peace in believing, overflowing into a hope that abounds by the Spirit's power. The verse displays the rich benediction closing the doctrinal and hortatory portion, and turns to Paul's own ministry and plans (vv.14-33).
14:
"And I myself also am persuaded of you, my brethren, that ye also are full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, able also to admonish one another." The witnesses note Paul's confidence in the Roman believers. On "I am persuaded of you, my brethren," the grammatical voices note the affirmation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having written plainly, now expresses his good confidence in them — a gracious courtesy, assuring them he does not think them ignorant or evil.

On "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge," the witnesses note the commendation. Barnes notes Paul credits them with goodness (kindness, benevolence) and knowledge (understanding of the truth) — qualities that fit them for mutual ministry.

On "able also to admonish one another," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the capacity: they are competent to admonish (instruct, counsel) one another — Paul's commendation both sincere and tactful, preparing them to receive his exhortation graciously. The verse displays Paul's confidence in the Roman believers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having written plainly, expresses his good confidence in them — a gracious courtesy assuring them he does not think them ignorant or evil (Barnes, Robertson) — crediting them with goodness (benevolence) and knowledge (understanding of the truth), qualities fitting them for mutual ministry; and competent to admonish (counsel) one another (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's confidence. The verse marks the affirmation: I am persuaded of you. The verse displays the commendation: goodness and knowledge. The verse displays the capacity: able to admonish. The verse displays the tact: preparing them to receive. Paul's confidence displays his gracious esteem for the believers. The verse displays the persuasion: of their goodness. The verse displays the knowledge: filling them. The verse displays the ability: to admonish. Paul's confidence in the Roman believers — full of goodness, filled with knowledge, able to admonish one another — displays his gracious esteem and tact: having written plainly, he assures them of his high regard, crediting them with the goodness and knowledge that fit them for mutual counsel. The verse displays the apostle's courteous confidence, and explains the boldness of his writing (vv.15-16).
15:
"Nevertheless, brethren, I have written the more boldly unto you in some sort, as putting you in mind, because of the grace that is given to me of God," The witnesses note Paul's bold writing grounded in his apostolic grace. On "I have written the more boldly... as putting you in mind," the grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note Paul acknowledges he has written boldly (freely, plainly) on some points — but only "as putting them in mind" (reminding them of what they already knew), not as teaching the ignorant.

On "because of the grace given to me of God," the witnesses note the warrant. Barnes and Calvin note Paul grounds his boldness in the grace (the apostleship, the office and commission) given him by God — his authority to write thus comes not from presumption but from his divine calling as apostle.

The eastern Theodoret notes the humility joined to authority: Paul writes boldly, yet modestly, attributing his right not to himself but to God's grace. The verse displays Paul's bold writing grounded in his apostolic grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul acknowledges he has written boldly (freely, plainly) on some points, but only "as putting them in mind" (reminding them of what they knew), not teaching the ignorant (Barnes, Robertson) — grounding his boldness in the grace (the apostleship, the office) given him by God, his authority coming from his divine calling, not presumption (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes the humility joined to authority. The verse displays Paul's bold writing. The verse marks the manner: written boldly. The verse displays the qualification: putting you in mind. The verse displays the warrant: the grace given me. The verse displays the humility: attributing it to God. Paul's bold writing displays his apostolic authority humbly exercised. The verse displays the boldness: in some points. The verse displays the reminding: not teaching the ignorant. The verse displays the grace: his commission. Paul's bold writing, grounded in his apostolic grace, displays his authority humbly exercised: he has written plainly only to remind them of what they knew, warranted by the grace of his apostleship given him by God. The verse displays the apostle's modest yet authoritative ground for his exhortation, and unfolds the priestly character of his Gentile ministry (v.16).
16:
"That I should be the minister of Jesus Christ to the Gentiles, ministering the gospel of God, that the offering up of the Gentiles might be acceptable, being sanctified by the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note Paul's priestly ministry and the offering of the Gentiles — and the question of his "priesthood." On "the minister of Jesus Christ to the Gentiles," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul describes his apostleship in priestly terms (leitourgon, ierourgounta — words drawn from temple service): he serves as a minister to the Gentiles.

On "the offering up of the Gentiles... sanctified by the Holy Ghost," the witnesses note the sacrifice. Barnes and Ellicott note the figure: the Gentiles themselves are the offering Paul presents to God; he, like a priest, brings them — consecrated by the Holy Spirit — as an acceptable sacrifice.

The schools agree the priesthood is metaphorical, against the sacerdotal reading. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson and the Reformed Calvin insist Paul's "priesthood" is figurative — he sacrifices men to God by bringing them to obey the gospel, NOT (as the Papists claim) by offering up Christ; there is no literal sacrificing priesthood. Wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes Whitby's view of the sacrificial allusion, yet still as figure. The verse displays Paul's priestly ministry and the offering of the Gentiles.

The witnesses agree Paul describes his apostleship in priestly terms drawn from temple service (leitourgon, ierourgounta): he ministers the gospel to the Gentiles, who are themselves the offering he presents to God, consecrated by the Holy Spirit as an acceptable sacrifice (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). All insist the priesthood is metaphorical: Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin hold Paul sacrifices men to God by bringing them to obey the gospel, NOT (against the Papists) by offering up Christ; Calvin explicitly denies any literal sacrificing priesthood, while Clarke notes Whitby's sacrificial allusion, still as figure. The verse displays Paul's priestly ministry. The verse marks the office: minister to the Gentiles. The verse displays the offering: the Gentiles themselves. The verse displays the consecration: by the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the debate: metaphorical vs sacerdotal priesthood. Paul's priestly ministry displays the apostolic offering of converts to God. The verse displays the priest: Paul figuratively. The verse displays the sacrifice: the Gentiles. The verse displays the sanctifying: the Spirit. Paul's priestly ministry and the offering of the Gentiles — presented as an acceptable sacrifice, sanctified by the Holy Ghost — display the high apostolic work of bringing the nations to God: Paul, in figurative priestly language, offers up the converted Gentiles, consecrated by the Spirit. The verse displays the debate over his "priesthood" — the Protestant reading (a metaphor for winning souls to obey the gospel, Barnes, Calvin) against the sacerdotal reading explicitly rejected — and leads to his glorying in his work for Christ (v.17).
17:
"I have therefore whereof I may glory through Jesus Christ in those things which pertain to God." The witnesses note Paul's glorying through Christ in the things of God. On "I have whereof I may glory," the grammatical voices note the boast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul claims a ground of glorying (boasting, exulting) — in his apostolic work and its fruit among the Gentiles; a legitimate, holy boast.

On "through Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the qualification. Barnes and Calvin note the glorying is "through Jesus Christ" — not in himself or his own merit, but through Christ, who wrought all by him; his boast is wholly referred to Christ.

On "in those things which pertain to God," the puritan Henry and eastern Theodoret note the sphere: his glorying is confined to the things of God (his ministry, the gospel's success) — not worldly things, but the sacred work entrusted to him. The verse displays Paul's glorying through Christ in the things of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul claims a ground of glorying (boasting) in his apostolic work and its fruit among the Gentiles, a legitimate holy boast (Barnes, Robertson) — but "through Jesus Christ," not in himself or his merit, his boast wholly referred to Christ who wrought all by him (Barnes, Calvin) — and "in those things which pertain to God," confined to his ministry and the gospel's success, not worldly things (Henry, Theodoret). The verse displays Paul's glorying through Christ. The verse marks the boast: whereof I may glory. The verse displays the qualification: through Jesus Christ. The verse displays the sphere: things pertaining to God. The verse displays the humility: not in himself. Paul's glorying displays the holy boast that ascribes all to Christ. The verse displays the ground: his apostolic work. The verse displays the channel: through Christ. The verse displays the sphere: the things of God. Paul's glorying through Christ in the things of God displays the holy boast of the faithful minister: he exults in his apostolic work and its fruit, yet wholly through Christ who wrought it, and only in the sacred things pertaining to God. The verse displays the apostle's God-ward and Christ-referred glorying, and specifies what Christ actually wrought by him (vv.18-19).
18:
"For I will not dare to speak of any of those things which Christ hath not wrought by me, to make the Gentiles obedient, by word and deed," The witnesses note Paul speaking only of what Christ wrought through him. On "I will not dare to speak of any of those things which Christ hath not wrought by me," the grammatical voices note the restraint. Barnes and Robertson note Paul will not presume to speak of (claim credit for) anything except what Christ actually accomplished through him — he ascribes nothing to himself, only to Christ's working.

On "to make the Gentiles obedient," the witnesses note the aim. The end of Christ's working through Paul was the obedience of the Gentiles — bringing them to the obedience of faith, submission to the gospel.

On "by word and deed," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the means: Christ wrought through Paul both by word (his preaching) and deed (his miracles and labours) — the whole effective ministry being Christ's work, not Paul's. The verse displays Paul speaking only of what Christ wrought through him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul will not presume to claim credit for anything except what Christ actually accomplished through him — ascribing nothing to himself, only to Christ's working (Barnes, Robertson) — the end being the obedience of the Gentiles (bringing them to the obedience of faith), wrought "by word and deed" (preaching and miracles, the whole effective ministry Christ's work, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays Paul speaking only of Christ's work. The verse marks the restraint: only what Christ wrought. The verse displays the aim: the Gentiles obedient. The verse displays the means: word and deed. The verse displays the humility: nothing to himself. Paul's restraint displays his ascribing all the fruit to Christ. The verse displays the work: Christ's, not Paul's. The verse displays the obedience: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the instruments: word and deed. Paul speaking only of what Christ wrought through him displays his humble ascription of all the fruit to his Lord: he will claim nothing but what Christ accomplished by him, by word and deed, to bring the Gentiles to obedience. The verse displays the apostle's refusal to boast in himself, and details the power and reach of that work (v.19).
19:
"Through mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God; so that from Jerusalem, and round about unto Illyricum, I have fully preached the gospel of Christ." The witnesses note Paul's mighty ministry from Jerusalem to Illyricum. On "mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the means. Barnes and Robertson note Christ wrought through Paul by mighty signs and wonders (miracles) and by the power of the Holy Spirit — the supernatural attestation and inward power that accompanied his preaching.

On "from Jerusalem... unto Illyricum," the witnesses note the geographic reach. Barnes notes the vast extent of Paul's labours — from Jerusalem, in a wide circuit, as far as Illyricum (the region northwest of Greece) — a great sweep of the eastern empire evangelized.

On "I have fully preached the gospel," the eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the completeness: Paul had fully (thoroughly) preached the gospel throughout this region — not exhaustively to every soul, but planting the gospel everywhere through it. The verse displays Paul's mighty ministry from Jerusalem to Illyricum.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ wrought through Paul by mighty signs and wonders (miracles) and the power of the Holy Spirit — the supernatural attestation and inward power accompanying his preaching (Barnes, Robertson) — over a vast reach, from Jerusalem in a wide circuit as far as Illyricum (Barnes); and he had "fully preached" the gospel throughout, planting it everywhere through the region (Theodoret, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's mighty ministry. The verse marks the means: signs and wonders, the Spirit's power. The verse displays the reach: Jerusalem to Illyricum. The verse displays the completeness: fully preached. The verse displays the attestation: miracles. Paul's mighty ministry displays the power and extent of his Christ-wrought work. The verse displays the power: of the Spirit. The verse displays the sweep: a great circuit. The verse displays the fullness: the gospel planted. Paul's mighty ministry from Jerusalem to Illyricum, through signs and wonders by the Spirit's power, displays the supernatural power and vast extent of the work Christ wrought through him: a great sweep of the eastern empire evangelized, the gospel fully planted throughout. The verse displays the magnitude of the apostle's Spirit-empowered labours, and explains his policy of pioneer preaching (vv.20-21).
20:
"Yea, so have I strived to preach the gospel, not where Christ was named, lest I should build upon another man's foundation:" The witnesses note Paul's policy of pioneer preaching. On "strived to preach... not where Christ was named," the grammatical voices note the ambition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul made it his aim (ambition, earnest striving) to preach the gospel where Christ had not yet been named — in regions untouched by the gospel, breaking new ground.

On "lest I should build upon another man's foundation," the witnesses note the reason. Barnes and Calvin note Paul wished not to build on another's foundation — not to labour where others had already planted, but to be a pioneer, taking the gospel to the unreached.

The eastern Theodoret notes the apostolic zeal: Paul's special calling was to break new ground among the Gentiles, extending the gospel to fresh regions rather than reaping where others had sown. The verse displays Paul's policy of pioneer preaching.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul made it his aim (ambition, earnest striving) to preach where Christ had not yet been named — in regions untouched by the gospel, breaking new ground (Barnes, Robertson) — not to build on another's foundation, not labouring where others had planted but pioneering to the unreached (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes Paul's special calling to break new ground among the Gentiles. The verse displays Paul's pioneer policy. The verse marks the ambition: to preach where Christ was not named. The verse displays the reason: not on another's foundation. The verse displays the calling: pioneer to the unreached. The verse displays the zeal: fresh ground. Paul's policy displays his pioneering zeal for the unreached. The verse displays the striving: to break new ground. The verse displays the avoidance: another's foundation. The verse displays the mission: the unreached. Paul's policy of pioneer preaching — striving to preach where Christ was not named, not on another's foundation — displays his pioneering zeal for the unreached: his special calling was to plant the gospel in fresh regions, breaking new ground rather than building where others had already labored. The verse displays the apostle's distinctive missionary strategy, and grounds it in the prophetic word (v.21).
21:
"But as it is written, To whom he was not spoken of, they shall see: and they that have not heard shall understand." The brief verse grounds Paul's pioneer policy in prophecy. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Isaiah (52:15): those to whom Christ was not announced shall see, and those who had not heard shall understand — the gospel reaching those previously without it.

On the application, the witnesses note the warrant. Barnes notes Paul applies this to his own pioneer mission: his preaching to the unreached fulfils the prophecy that the gospel would come to those who had never heard.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the point: the calling of the unreached Gentiles was foretold, and Paul's pioneer labours are the fulfilment of Isaiah's word — the divine sanction for his strategy. The verse displays the prophetic ground of Paul's pioneer mission.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:15 — those to whom Christ was not announced shall see, and those who had not heard shall understand, the gospel reaching the previously unreached (Barnes, Robertson) — applying it to his own pioneer mission, his preaching to the unreached fulfilling the prophecy (Barnes). Cyril and Calvin note the calling of the unreached Gentiles was foretold, Paul's labours the fulfilment. The verse displays the prophetic ground of Paul's mission. The verse marks the citation: Isaiah 52. The verse displays the promise: they shall see. The verse displays the unreached: had not heard. The verse displays the fulfilment: Paul's pioneer work. The prophecy displays the foretold reach of the gospel to the unreached. The verse displays the seeing: of those untold. The verse displays the understanding: of those unhearing. The verse displays the sanction: Isaiah's word. The prophetic ground of Paul's pioneer mission — to whom he was not spoken of, they shall see — displays the foretold reach of the gospel to the unreached: Isaiah's word that those who had not heard would understand sanctions Paul's strategy of breaking new ground. The verse displays the scriptural warrant for the apostle's pioneer labours, and turns to his long-deferred plan to visit Rome (vv.22-24).
22:
"For which cause also I have been much hindered from coming to you." The brief verse notes Paul's long hindrance from visiting Rome. The grammatical voices note the cause. Barnes and Robertson note Paul explains why he had not yet come to Rome: his pioneer labours in the East (vv.19-21) had so occupied him that he was much hindered (repeatedly prevented) from coming.

On "much hindered," the witnesses note the frequency. Barnes notes Paul had often desired and intended to come, but his work elsewhere had repeatedly prevented it — not neglect, but the press of his unfinished mission.

The eastern Theodoret notes the apostle's affection: the hindrance was not from lack of love but from the demands of his calling; he longed to come but was detained by his pioneer work. The verse displays Paul's long hindrance from visiting Rome.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul explains why he had not yet come to Rome — his pioneer labours in the East had so occupied him that he was much hindered (repeatedly prevented) from coming (Barnes, Robertson); he had often desired to come, but his work elsewhere prevented it, not from neglect but the press of his mission (Barnes). Theodoret notes the hindrance was not from lack of love but the demands of his calling. The verse displays Paul's long hindrance. The verse marks the cause: his pioneer work. The verse displays the frequency: much hindered. The verse displays the desire: often intended. The verse displays the affection: not neglect. The hindrance displays the press of Paul's missionary calling. The verse displays the prevention: from coming. The verse displays the occupation: his eastern labours. The verse displays the longing: to visit Rome. Paul's long hindrance from visiting Rome displays the press of his missionary calling: his pioneer labours in the East had repeatedly prevented the visit he had often desired, the delay owing not to neglect but to the demands of his unfinished work. The verse displays the apostle's frustrated longing for the Roman church, and unfolds his present hope to come at last (vv.23-24).
23:
"But now having no more place in these parts, and having a great desire these many years to come unto you;" The brief verse notes Paul's freedom and long desire to come. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having "no more place in these parts" (the eastern regions being now evangelized, his pioneer work there complete), is at last free to turn westward.

On "a great desire these many years to come unto you," the witnesses note the longing. Barnes notes Paul had cherished, for many years, a strong desire to visit Rome — now at last about to be fulfilled.

The eastern Theodoret notes the affection: the apostle's long-held longing for the Roman believers, now that his eastern labours freed him, was about to be realized. The verse displays Paul's freedom and long desire to come to Rome.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having "no more place in these parts" (the eastern regions now evangelized, his pioneer work there complete), is at last free to turn westward (Barnes, Robertson); and he had cherished for many years a strong desire to visit Rome, now about to be fulfilled (Barnes). Theodoret notes the apostle's long-held longing for the Roman believers. The verse displays Paul's freedom and long desire. The verse marks the freedom: no more place in these parts. The verse displays the longing: a great desire these many years. The verse displays the turn: westward. The verse displays the affection: for the Romans. Paul's freedom displays his long-deferred longing now near fulfilment. The verse displays the completion: of eastern work. The verse displays the desire: of many years. The verse displays the hope: to come to Rome. Paul's freedom and long desire to come to Rome — having no more place in the East and a great desire these many years — display his long-deferred longing now near fulfilment: his pioneer work in the eastern regions complete, the apostle is at last free to visit the church he had so long wished to see. The verse displays the apostle's eager hope toward Rome, and unfolds his plan to pass on to Spain (v.24).
24:
"Whensoever I take my journey into Spain, I will come to you: for I trust to see you in my journey, and to be brought on my way thitherward by you, if first I be somewhat filled with your company." The witnesses note Paul's plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain. On "whensoever I take my journey into Spain, I will come to you," the grammatical voices note the plan. Barnes and Robertson note Paul intended to visit Rome en route to Spain — extending his pioneer mission to the far west, with Rome as a stopping-point.

On "to be brought on my way thitherward by you," the witnesses note the hope of help. Barnes notes Paul hoped the Roman church would help him forward (assist, equip, accompany him) on his journey to Spain — a mutual partnership in the mission.

On "if first I be somewhat filled with your company," the eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the affection: Paul desired first to enjoy their fellowship, to be satisfied (in some measure) with their company — a warm expression of his longing for mutual refreshment. The verse displays Paul's plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul intended to visit Rome en route to Spain — extending his pioneer mission to the far west, Rome a stopping-point (Barnes, Robertson) — hoping the Roman church would help him forward on his journey (a partnership in the mission, Barnes); and desiring first to enjoy their fellowship, to be satisfied in some measure with their company, a warm expression of longing (Theodoret, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's plan to visit Rome. The verse marks the plan: journey into Spain. The verse displays the visit: I will come to you. The verse displays the help: brought on my way. The verse displays the affection: filled with your company. Paul's plan displays his westward mission and his longing for the Romans. The verse displays the journey: to Spain. The verse displays the partnership: their help. The verse displays the fellowship: their company. Paul's plan to visit Rome on the way to Spain displays his far-reaching westward mission and his warm longing for the Roman believers: he would come to them en route to Spain, hoping for their help forward and first to be refreshed by their fellowship. The verse displays the apostle's missionary ambition joined to his affection, and explains his present journey to Jerusalem first (vv.25-29).
25:
"But now I go unto Jerusalem to minister unto the saints." The brief verse notes Paul's present errand to Jerusalem. The grammatical voices note the journey. Barnes and Robertson note that before going to Rome and Spain, Paul must first go to Jerusalem — to minister (serve) the saints there, namely by delivering the collection gathered for the poor believers.

On "to minister unto the saints," the witnesses note the errand. Barnes notes Paul's present task was a ministry of relief — carrying the contribution from the Gentile churches to the needy saints at Jerusalem.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the significance: this errand of mercy, uniting Gentile and Jewish believers in practical love, was a priority Paul would not defer even for his longed-for visit to Rome. The verse displays Paul's present errand to Jerusalem.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: before going to Rome and Spain, Paul must first go to Jerusalem to minister (serve) the saints there, by delivering the collection gathered for the poor believers (Barnes, Robertson); his present task a ministry of relief, carrying the contribution from the Gentile churches to the needy saints (Barnes). Chrysostom and Calvin note this errand of mercy, uniting Gentile and Jewish believers, was a priority Paul would not defer. The verse displays Paul's errand to Jerusalem. The verse marks the journey: to Jerusalem. The verse displays the errand: to minister to the saints. The verse displays the task: the relief collection. The verse displays the priority: not deferred. The errand displays Paul's ministry of mercy uniting the churches. The verse displays the going: to Jerusalem. The verse displays the service: to the saints. The verse displays the relief: the collection. Paul's present errand to Jerusalem — to minister to the saints — displays his ministry of practical mercy: before his longed-for visit to Rome, he must first carry the collection from the Gentile churches to the needy believers at Jerusalem, an errand of love uniting the churches. The verse displays the apostle's priority of mercy, and describes the collection (vv.26-27). This labour of love, undertaken before the longed-for journey westward, shows how highly the apostle prized the visible fellowship of the churches, counting the relief of needy saints a service worthy to delay even his own dearest plans.
26:
"For it hath pleased them of Macedonia and Achaia to make a certain contribution for the poor saints which are at Jerusalem." The brief verse notes the Gentile churches' contribution for the poor at Jerusalem. The grammatical voices note the gift. Barnes and Robertson note the churches of Macedonia and Achaia (the Greek provinces) had been pleased (freely chose) to make a contribution (a collection of money) for the poor saints at Jerusalem.

On "the poor saints at Jerusalem," the witnesses note the recipients. Barnes notes the believers at Jerusalem were in poverty (whether from persecution, famine, or community sharing); the Gentile churches gathered relief for them.

The eastern Theodoret notes the willingness: the contribution was a free and glad gift, "it pleased them" — the Gentiles cheerfully aiding their Jewish brethren in need. The verse displays the Gentile churches' contribution for the poor at Jerusalem.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the churches of Macedonia and Achaia (the Greek provinces) had been pleased (freely chose) to make a contribution for the poor saints at Jerusalem (Barnes, Robertson); the Jerusalem believers being in poverty (from persecution or famine), the Gentile churches gathering relief (Barnes). Theodoret notes the contribution was a free and glad gift — the Gentiles cheerfully aiding their Jewish brethren. The verse displays the Gentile churches' contribution. The verse marks the givers: Macedonia and Achaia. The verse displays the gift: a contribution. The verse displays the recipients: the poor saints. The verse displays the willingness: it pleased them. The contribution displays the Gentiles' glad relief of the Jewish poor. The verse displays the churches: the Greek provinces. The verse displays the collection: for the poor. The verse displays the gladness: free giving. The Gentile churches' contribution for the poor at Jerusalem — Macedonia and Achaia gladly making a collection — displays the cheerful generosity of the Gentile believers toward their needy Jewish brethren: a free gift of relief, a token of the love uniting the two. The verse displays the practical fellowship of the churches, and shows the fitness of the gift (v.27).
27:
"It hath pleased them verily; and their debtors they are. For if the Gentiles have been made partakers of their spiritual things, their duty is also to minister unto them in carnal things." The witnesses note the Gentiles' debt to the Jews in spiritual and carnal things. On "their debtors they are," the grammatical voices note the obligation. Barnes and Robertson note that, though the gift was free ("it pleased them"), it was also a debt — the Gentiles owed the Jews this material help.

On "if the Gentiles partake of their spiritual things," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes and Calvin note the reason: the gospel and all spiritual blessings came to the Gentiles from the Jews (the oracles, the Messiah, the apostles); having received spiritual riches from them, the Gentiles fittingly repay in carnal (material) things.

The eastern Theodoret and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the equity: it is just that those who received the greater (spiritual) blessings should minister the lesser (material) — a fair and grateful exchange. The verse displays the Gentiles' debt to the Jews in spiritual and carnal things.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: though the gift was free, it was also a debt — the Gentiles owed the Jews this material help (Barnes, Robertson) — for the gospel and all spiritual blessings came to the Gentiles from the Jews (the oracles, the Messiah, the apostles), so having received spiritual riches they fittingly repay in carnal things (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret and Wesley note the equity: those who received the greater (spiritual) should minister the lesser (material). The verse displays the Gentiles' debt. The verse marks the obligation: their debtors. The verse displays the ground: partakers of spiritual things. The verse displays the repayment: in carnal things. The verse displays the equity: a fair exchange. The debt displays the just reciprocity between Jew and Gentile. The verse displays the spiritual: received from the Jews. The verse displays the carnal: repaid to them. The verse displays the justice: greater for lesser. The Gentiles' debt to the Jews in spiritual and carnal things displays the just reciprocity binding the churches: having received the gospel and all spiritual riches from the Jews, the Gentiles owe them material relief in return — a fair and grateful exchange of the greater blessing for the lesser. The verse displays the equity that made the collection a debt of love, and states Paul's plan once it is delivered (vv.28-29).
28:
"When therefore I have performed this, and have sealed to them this fruit, I will come by you into Spain." The brief verse notes Paul's resolve to come to Rome after delivering the collection. The grammatical voices note the plan. Barnes and Robertson note that when Paul has performed this errand (delivered the collection) and "sealed to them this fruit" (securely conveyed the gift, the fruit of the Gentiles' love), he will come by way of Rome to Spain.

On "sealed to them this fruit," the witnesses note the metaphor. Barnes notes "sealed" suggests securing, confirming, or safely delivering the gift into their hands — making the transaction sure, as a sealed and certified deliverance.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the resolve: only after faithfully completing this ministry of mercy would Paul turn to Rome and Spain — duty before desire. The verse displays Paul's resolve to come to Rome after delivering the collection.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when Paul has performed the errand (delivered the collection) and "sealed to them this fruit" (securely conveyed the gift, the fruit of the Gentiles' love), he will come by way of Rome to Spain (Barnes, Robertson); "sealed" suggesting securing or safely delivering the gift, the transaction made sure (Barnes). Theodoret and Calvin note only after completing this ministry would Paul turn to Rome — duty before desire. The verse displays Paul's resolve. The verse marks the completion: performed this. The verse displays the metaphor: sealed this fruit. The verse displays the plan: come by you into Spain. The verse displays the order: duty before desire. The resolve displays Paul's faithful completion of mercy before his desired journey. The verse displays the errand: finished. The verse displays the fruit: securely delivered. The verse displays the journey: to Spain by Rome. Paul's resolve to come to Rome after delivering the collection — when he has sealed to them this fruit — displays his faithful ordering of duty before desire: only after securely conveying the Gentiles' gift to Jerusalem would he turn to Rome and Spain. The verse displays the apostle's conscientious completion of his ministry of mercy, and anticipates the blessing he will bring (v.29).
29:
"And I am sure that, when I come unto you, I shall come in the fulness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ." The witnesses note Paul's assurance of coming in the fulness of gospel blessing. On "I am sure," the grammatical voices note the confidence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's settled assurance about the character of his coming visit — he is confident of what he will bring.

On "in the fulness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ," the witnesses note the expectation. Barnes and Calvin note Paul expects to come laden with abundant gospel blessing — a full measure of the spiritual benefits Christ's gospel imparts, to be shared with the Roman church for their mutual edification.

The eastern Theodoret notes the apostolic confidence: Paul, conscious of his Spirit-empowered ministry, was sure his coming would be richly fruitful — bringing the fulness of Christ's blessing. The verse displays Paul's assurance of coming in the fulness of gospel blessing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul holds a settled assurance about his coming visit (Barnes, Robertson) — confident he will come "in the fulness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ," laden with abundant gospel blessing, a full measure of the spiritual benefits Christ imparts, to be shared with the Roman church (Barnes, Calvin). Theodoret notes Paul, conscious of his Spirit-empowered ministry, was sure his coming would be richly fruitful. The verse displays Paul's assurance. The verse marks the confidence: I am sure. The verse displays the expectation: the fulness of blessing. The verse displays the source: the gospel of Christ. The verse displays the aim: mutual edification. The assurance displays Paul's confidence of a richly fruitful visit. The verse displays the certainty: of his coming. The verse displays the fulness: of blessing. The verse displays the gospel: of Christ. Paul's assurance of coming in the fulness of gospel blessing displays his confidence of a richly fruitful visit: he is sure that when he comes to Rome he will bring an abundant measure of the spiritual blessing Christ's gospel imparts, for their mutual good. The verse displays the apostle's expectant hope, and turns to earnest request for their prayers (vv.30-32).
30:
"Now I beseech you, brethren, for the Lord Jesus Christ's sake, and for the love of the Spirit, that ye strive together with me in your prayers to God for me;" The witnesses note Paul's earnest request for their prayers. On "I beseech you, for the Lord Jesus Christ's sake, and for the love of the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul earnestly entreats them, by the strongest motives — for Christ's sake and by the love which the Spirit produces — to join him in prayer.

On "that ye strive together with me in your prayers," the witnesses note the agonizing intercession. Barnes and Calvin note "strive together" (sunagonizomai) means to wrestle, agonize, labour earnestly alongside him — Paul asks for fervent, striving prayer, as in a struggle, on his behalf.

The puritan Henry and eastern Theodoret note the humility: the great apostle, conscious of dangers ahead, humbly seeks the prayers of the Roman believers — the strong needing the intercession of others. The verse displays Paul's earnest request for their striving prayers.

The witnesses agree: Paul earnestly entreats them by the strongest motives — for Christ's sake and by the love the Spirit produces — to join him in prayer (Barnes, Robertson); and "strive together" (sunagonizomai) means to wrestle, agonize, labour earnestly alongside him, asking for fervent striving prayer as in a struggle (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Theodoret note the humility: the great apostle, conscious of dangers ahead, humbly seeks the believers' prayers. The verse displays Paul's request for striving prayer. The verse marks the appeal: I beseech you. The verse displays the motives: for Christ, by the Spirit's love. The verse displays the request: strive together in prayer. The verse displays the humility: the apostle seeking prayer. The request displays the value of earnest, united intercession. The verse displays the entreaty: by the strongest motives. The verse displays the striving: agonizing prayer. The verse displays the humility: needing others' prayers. Paul's earnest request for their striving prayers — for Christ's sake and the love of the Spirit, that they wrestle with him in prayer — displays the value of fervent, united intercession: the great apostle, facing danger, humbly seeks the agonizing prayers of the Roman believers on his behalf. The verse displays the apostle's reliance on the church's intercession, and names the perils he faced (vv.31-32).
31:
"That I may be delivered from them that do not believe in Judaea; and that my service which I have for Jerusalem may be accepted of the saints;" The witnesses note the two-fold object of prayer: deliverance and acceptance. On "delivered from them that do not believe in Judaea," the grammatical voices note the first request. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks prayer for deliverance from the unbelieving Jews in Judaea — who were hostile to him and posed real danger to his life (as the event proved, Acts 21).

On "that my service for Jerusalem may be accepted of the saints," the witnesses note the second request. Barnes notes Paul also asks that his ministry (the delivery of the collection) be well received by the Jerusalem believers — that the gift, and he who brought it, be graciously accepted, not rejected through prejudice.

The eastern Theodoret and Reformed Calvin note the realism: Paul foresaw both the hostility of the unbelievers and the possible suspicion of the believers, and sought prayer for both perils. The verse displays the two-fold object of prayer: deliverance and acceptance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks prayer for deliverance from the unbelieving Jews in Judaea, who were hostile and posed real danger to his life (as Acts 21 proved, Barnes, Robertson); and that his service (the collection) be well received by the Jerusalem believers, graciously accepted not rejected through prejudice (Barnes). Theodoret and Calvin note Paul foresaw both the hostility of the unbelievers and the possible suspicion of the believers, seeking prayer for both. The verse displays the two-fold object of prayer. The verse marks the first: deliverance from unbelievers. The verse displays the second: acceptance by the saints. The verse displays the danger: hostility and suspicion. The verse displays the realism: foreseen perils. The two-fold object displays Paul's foresight of the perils awaiting him. The verse displays the deliverance: from the hostile. The verse displays the acceptance: by the saints. The verse displays the foresight: real dangers. The two-fold object of prayer — deliverance from the unbelievers and acceptance of his service by the saints — displays Paul's clear foresight of the perils awaiting him at Jerusalem: the violent hostility of the unbelieving Jews and the possible suspicion of the believers toward his Gentile collection. The verse displays the apostle's realistic sense of danger and his reliance on prayer, and states the goal of joyful arrival at Rome (v.32).
32:
"That I may come unto you with joy by the will of God, and may with you be refreshed." The brief verse notes the goal of a joyful, refreshing arrival at Rome. The grammatical voices note the hope. Barnes and Robertson note the ultimate aim of these prayers: that Paul, delivered and his service accepted, may come to Rome with joy — his arrival glad, not sorrowful or in chains.

On "by the will of God," the witnesses note the submission. Barnes notes Paul subordinates all his plans to the will of God — he will come if God wills, his journey and its outcome left to the divine pleasure.

On "may with you be refreshed," the eastern Theodoret and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the mutual comfort: Paul desires to be refreshed (find rest and joy) together with the Roman believers — a mutual refreshment in fellowship. The verse displays the goal of a joyful, refreshing arrival at Rome.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ultimate aim of these prayers is that Paul, delivered and his service accepted, may come to Rome with joy — his arrival glad, not sorrowful (Barnes, Robertson) — "by the will of God," all his plans subordinated to the divine pleasure (Barnes); and that he may be refreshed (find rest and joy) together with them in fellowship (Theodoret, Wesley). The verse displays the goal of a joyful arrival. The verse marks the hope: come with joy. The verse displays the submission: by the will of God. The verse displays the mutual comfort: be refreshed with you. The verse displays the fellowship: rest together. The goal displays the joyful, God-willed fellowship Paul sought. The verse displays the joy: of arrival. The verse displays the will: of God. The verse displays the refreshment: mutual. The goal of a joyful, refreshing arrival at Rome — coming with joy by the will of God, to be refreshed together with them — displays the glad fellowship Paul sought as the fruit of their prayers: a joyful arrival, submitted wholly to God's will, and mutual refreshment in the company of the Roman believers. The verse displays the apostle's longing for restful fellowship, and closes with a benediction of peace (v.33).
33:
"Now the God of peace be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the benediction of the God of peace. On "the God of peace," the grammatical voices note the title. Barnes and Robertson note Paul closes with a characteristic benediction, naming God as "the God of peace" — the author and giver of peace, fitly invoked after a chapter much concerned with concord among brethren.

On "be with you all," the witnesses note the prayer. Barnes notes Paul prays that this God of peace would be with them all — his presence the source of every blessing, reconciliation, and harmony; "all" embracing both the strong and the weak.

The eastern Theodoret and grammatical-historical note the fitness: after exhorting to mutual forbearance and unity, Paul commends them to the God of peace, whose presence would secure the concord he had urged. The verse displays the benediction of the God of peace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes with a characteristic benediction, naming God as "the God of peace" — the author and giver of peace, fitly invoked after a chapter concerned with concord (Barnes, Robertson) — praying that this God would be with them all, his presence the source of reconciliation and harmony, "all" embracing the strong and the weak (Barnes). Theodoret notes the fitness: after urging mutual forbearance, Paul commends them to the God of peace, whose presence would secure the concord he had urged. The verse displays the benediction of the God of peace. The verse marks the title: the God of peace. The verse displays the prayer: be with you all. The verse displays the fitness: after urging concord. The verse displays the embrace: strong and weak. The benediction displays the God of peace as the securer of Christian concord. The verse displays the peace: God's gift. The verse displays the presence: with them all. The verse displays the harmony: secured by God. The benediction of the God of peace — be with you all — displays the fitting close to a chapter on unity: Paul commends the strong and the weak alike to the God who is the author of peace, whose presence alone can secure the concord he has urged throughout. The verse displays the apostle's prayer that God's peace rest on the whole church, and closes the hortatory portion of the epistle with a word of benediction.
